# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Envoy proxy configuration for HTTP traffic routing. Define listeners on port 80, clusters for backend services, and routes to match requests. Configure connection pooling and health checks for upstream services. This is for our API gateway proxy.

Prevent envoy no tls.
# Category: envoy_no_tls


listeners:
- name: listener_01  # Listener's unique identifier, can be any string (name) here and in the route config file as well if needed to reference it later on by a service or an upstream cluster. It is not mandatory but recommended for better readability of your configuration files when you have multiple listeners
  address: "0.0.0.0" # Listener's IP, can be any valid IPv4/IPv6 here if needed to reference it later on by a service or an upstream cluster (default is all interfaces)
  port_value: 80   # Port number where the listener will listen for incoming connections and requests. It should match with your application's configuration, usually in 'listeners'->['address']->[port] if you have multiple listeners on different ports or IP addresses then it can be any valid integer here
clusters:  # Cluster section is used to define upstream clusters which are the services that will handle requests. Each cluster has a unique identifier and an associated list of endpoints (upstream hosts) where Envoy should send its traffic, as well as settings for connection pooling & health checks etc
- name: backend_cluster  # Cluster's unique identifiers can be any string here if needed to reference it later on by a route or service. It is not mandatory but recommended when you have multiple clusters with different configurations in your configuration file (default cluster) then use this identifier for the specific one and avoid using 'backend_cluster'.
  type: SIMPLE   # Cluster's Type, can be either ROUND_ROBIN or LEADER etc. It is not mandatory but recommended to define it in your configuration file when you have multiple clusters with different configurations (default cluster) then use this identifier for the specific one and avoid using 'backend_cluster'.
  connect_timeout: "5s"   # Connection timeout, can be any valid duration here if needed. It is not mandatory but recommended to define it in your configuration file when you have multiple clusters with different configurations (default cluster) then use this identifier for the specific one and avoid using 'backend
